title: MutableLists
description: Lists that can dynamically grow and shrink.

slides.title: Unlocking the List 🔓
slides.content:
## Editable Containers

If you need a list that you can dynamically edit (adding, removing, or changing items over time), you must use a **MutableList** instead of a standard List.

slides.title: Creating a MutableList ➕
slides.content: To create one, use `mutableListOf()`. Now, all of the editing functions become permanently unlocked for you to use!

quizData.question: What happens when you call `.add("Grapes")` on a `MutableList`?

options.text: It adds 'Grapes' to the very beginning.
It appends 'Grapes' to the very end of the list.

quizData.explanation: By default, the `.add()` function appends the new item to the very end of the line, increasing the list size.

slides.title: Why two types? 🛡️
slides.content:
## Safety First (Again)

Why does Kotlin split Lists into `listOf` and `mutableListOf`? Why not make them all editable like Python or Javascript?

**Safety.** If you pass an editable list into a complex function, you risk that function maliciously deleting or corrupting your data. A read-only `listOf` guarantees 100% that your data will never be tampered with.
